stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 13-year-old boy with three months of recurrent right-sided unilateral epistaxis, often requiring ED visits with packing. The episodes have become more frequent and are accompanied by progressive right-sided nasal obstruction. He has no easy bruising, no menorrhagia equivalent in family, and his bleeding screen at the last admission was normal. On anterior rhinoscopy you can see a smooth, lobulated, vascular-looking mass behind the right middle turbinate. He has no other red-flag symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Smooth lobulated reddish-purple mass posterior to right middle turbinate, no neck lymphadenopathy, no cranial nerve deficits, no proptosis
data_table.Investigations: FBC and coagulation screen normal; nasal endoscopy as described

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: In-office biopsy through the nasal cavity for histological diagnosis prior to any imaging
options.rationale: Direct biopsy of suspected juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma can cause life-threatening haemorrhage; imaging confirms the diagnosis before any tissue sampling. (Source: RANZCO/AAOHNS)

options.text: Repeat full blood count and coagulation screen in two weeks before further investigation
options.rationale: The history of unilateral nasal mass plus progressive obstruction points to a structural lesion, not coagulopathy; further haematology testing will not capture this diagnosis. (Source: RANZCO/AAOHNS)

options.text: Contrast-enhanced MRI of the nasopharynx and skull base before any tissue biopsy is taken
options.rationale: Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma in an adolescent male is a vascular tumour that bleeds catastrophically on biopsy; contrast MRI defines extent and intracranial spread, followed by preoperative angiography and embolisation. (Source: RANZCO and AAOHNS combined head and neck guidance)

options.text: Plain X-ray of the post-nasal space to estimate soft-tissue density before any cross-section
options.rationale: Plain films are insensitive for soft-tissue tumours of the post-nasal space and cannot define skull-base involvement. (Source: RANZCO/AAOHNS)

options.text: Empirical course of intranasal mometasone for six weeks before reimaging or specialist review
options.rationale: Steroid sprays treat allergic rhinitis, not a vascular nasopharyngeal mass, and delay definitive diagnosis. (Source: RANZCO/AAOHNS)

explanation.summary: Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma is a benign but locally aggressive vascular tumour of adolescent males that presents with recurrent unilateral epistaxis and progressive nasal obstruction. A vascular nasopharyngeal mass should never be biopsied without imaging because of the risk of catastrophic haemorrhage. Contrast MRI defines extent and skull-base spread, followed by angiography, preoperative embolisation and endoscopic resection.
key_points: Adolescent male + unilateral epistaxis + nasal obstruction = consider JNA.
Image before any biopsy.
Pre-op angiography and embolisation reduces operative blood loss.
explanation.pearls: Never biopsy a smooth red mass behind the middle turbinate in an adolescent male without imaging first; embolise within 24 to 48 hours of resection to minimise revascularisation.

stem: You are the paediatric haematology registrar reviewing an 8-month-old boy of Greek heritage referred for progressive pallor, hepatosplenomegaly and faltering growth from 4 months of age. Newborn screening detected an elevated HbF and follow-up parental studies showed both parents to be beta-thalassaemia trait carriers. Confirmatory testing in the baby has shown two beta zero mutations on molecular genetic testing. He is now feeding poorly, listless and has hepatosplenomegaly extending 4 cm below the costal margins.

data_table.Examination: Marked pallor, mild scleral icterus, hepatomegaly 4 cm, splenomegaly 4 cm, frontal bossing, growth dropping across centiles
data_table.Investigations: Hb 58 g/L, MCV 62 fL, reticulocytes 10%, HbF 90% on HPLC, HbA absent, ferritin 80 micrograms/L, LDH elevated, bilirubin 35 micromol/L (unconjugated)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Commence hydroxyurea 15 mg/kg daily to induce fetal haemoglobin and avoid regular transfusion
options.rationale: Hydroxyurea is established in sickle cell disease and used in some intermediate thalassaemias; it is not first-line in transfusion-dependent beta-thalassaemia major. (Source: HSANZ)

options.text: Commence oral folic acid 5 mg daily and review in six weeks before considering any transfusion
options.rationale: Folate alone does not correct the underlying defect; in transfusion-dependent thalassaemia, folate is an adjunct, not a definitive treatment, and this child is already symptomatic. (Source: HSANZ)

options.text: Defer transfusion until haemoglobin falls below 50 g/L to limit cumulative iron loading risk
options.rationale: Withholding transfusion until profound anaemia leads to extramedullary haematopoiesis, severe skeletal changes and growth failure; modern regimens transfuse at pre-transfusion Hb 95 to 105 g/L. (Source: HSANZ)

options.text: Start regular transfusion targeting pre-transfusion Hb 95 to 105 g/L; chelate when ferritin rises
options.rationale: Confirmed beta-thalassaemia major with symptomatic anaemia, organomegaly and faltering growth meets criteria to start regular transfusion at a pre-transfusion Hb of 95 to 105 g/L; chelation is started later when ferritin rises. (Source: HSANZ: Thalassaemia in Australia)

options.text: Refer immediately for splenectomy as the primary measure to reduce transfusion requirements
options.rationale: Splenectomy is deferred until age over 5 years and reserved for excessive transfusion requirements, given infection and thrombosis risks. (Source: HSANZ)

explanation.summary: Beta-thalassaemia major presents in the second half of the first year as fetal haemoglobin production wanes, with progressive anaemia, hepatosplenomegaly and faltering growth. Once the diagnosis is confirmed and the child is symptomatic, regular transfusion at pre-transfusion Hb 95 to 105 g/L suppresses ineffective erythropoiesis and supports growth. Iron chelation is added when ferritin exceeds about 1000 micrograms/L, typically after 10 to 20 transfusions.
key_points: Start regular transfusion when symptoms, growth failure or organomegaly emerge.
Pre-transfusion target Hb 95 to 105 g/L.
Iron chelation begins around 10-20 transfusions or ferritin over 1000.
explanation.pearls: Curative haematopoietic stem cell transplant from an HLA-matched sibling should be discussed early in beta-thalassaemia major to capture the best-outcome window before iron overload.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 7-year-old girl whose teacher and parents have noticed dozens of episodes per day where she stops, stares, blinks rapidly and is unresponsive for 5 to 10 seconds, then resumes activity. She has had no convulsive seizures, no postictal phase, and her development is normal. Hyperventilation in clinic for 3 minutes triggers a typical event. She has no family history of epilepsy and no behavioural concerns. The EEG shows generalised 3 Hz spike-and-wave with normal background.

data_table.Investigations: EEG: 3 Hz generalised spike-and-wave provoked by hyperventilation, normal awake and sleep background; MRI brain not indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line medication?

options.text: Carbamazepine titrated to 15 mg/kg per day in two divided doses as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Carbamazepine can aggravate absence seizures and is contraindicated in idiopathic generalised epilepsies. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Lamotrigine titrated slowly over six weeks to 5 mg/kg per day as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is third-line for absence epilepsy; slow titration and lower response rates make it inferior to ethosuximide or valproate as initial therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Sodium valproate titrated to 20 mg/kg per day in two divided doses as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Valproate is effective in childhood absence epilepsy but is second-line because of greater attentional side-effects in the CAE trial and the teratogenic risk that becomes relevant later in adolescence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Levetiracetam titrated to 40 mg/kg per day in two divided doses as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Levetiracetam is not licensed for absence epilepsy in monotherapy and has lower efficacy than ethosuximide or valproate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Ethosuximide titrated to 15 to 40 mg/kg per day in two divided doses as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Ethosuximide is first-line for typical childhood absence epilepsy because it has superior efficacy and a better cognitive profile than valproate in childhood absence epilepsy (CAE) head-to-head trials. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology and Cochrane review of CAE)

explanation.summary: Childhood absence epilepsy is an idiopathic generalised epilepsy that begins between 4 and 10 years with multiple daily brief unresponsive episodes triggered by hyperventilation, and a characteristic 3 Hz generalised spike-and-wave EEG. Ethosuximide is the AU first-line monotherapy. Carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, gabapentin and tiagabine should be avoided because they can worsen absences. Most children remit before puberty.
key_points: Ethosuximide first-line, valproate second-line.
Avoid carbamazepine (worsens absences).
Hyperventilation provocation in clinic supports diagnosis.
explanation.pearls: If a child with apparent CAE has additional myoclonic jerks or GTCS, suspect juvenile myoclonic epilepsy and switch from ethosuximide to valproate or lamotrigine.

stem: You are the GP seeing a 4-year-old boy with three weeks of persistent night pain in both legs, increasing pallor and easy bruising. His mother brought him today because he is now refusing to walk in the mornings, and she noticed petechiae on his shins. He has had two low-grade fevers over the last week. There is no recent trauma, no joint swelling and no rash beyond the petechiae. He is otherwise alert and engages with you in clinic.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4°C, HR 130, RR 24, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 95/58
data_table.Examination: Pallor, scattered petechiae on shins, palpable cervical and inguinal lymph nodes 1.5 cm, hepatomegaly 3 cm below costal margin, no joint swelling, no neurology
data_table.Investigations: Hb 70 g/L, WCC 24 x10^9/L with 70% blasts on film, platelets 18 x10^9/L, neutrophils 0.3 x10^9/L, LDH 1800 U/L, urate 0.55 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Refer to orthopaedics for suspected septic arthritis of the lower limbs before further workup
options.rationale: There is no joint swelling, warmth or limp localisation; the bicompartmental nightly pain in a child with pancytopenia points to marrow infiltration, not infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Limp)

options.text: Commence empirical oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily and outpatient haematology in two weeks
options.rationale: Empirical steroids before tissue diagnosis can mask the marrow picture and delay accurate classification of the leukaemia subtype. (Source: eviQ)

options.text: Request outpatient FBC and bone biochemistry in one week with GP review of the results
options.rationale: The blood film already shows blasts; deferring action by a week risks tumour lysis, severe sepsis with neutropenia, or bleeding complications. (Source: eviQ)

options.text: Same-day paediatric oncology contact and direct transfer for tumour-lysis prophylaxis and marrow
options.rationale: Pancytopenia, peripheral blasts, hepatomegaly, lymphadenopathy and biochemical evidence of high cell turnover suggest acute leukaemia; immediate transfer for tumour-lysis prophylaxis (hyperhydration, allopurinol or rasburicase) and bone marrow assessment is mandatory. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL induction)

options.text: Commence oral ibuprofen for presumed growing pains and arrange GP review in one week
options.rationale: Night pain that wakes the child, with pallor, petechiae and hepatomegaly, is incompatible with benign growing pains and ibuprofen could worsen bleeding. (Source: RCH CPG: Limp)

explanation.summary: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia commonly presents with bone pain, pallor, petechiae or bruising, hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy, and recurrent low-grade fevers. Pancytopenia with circulating blasts requires same-day discussion with paediatric oncology and admission for tumour-lysis prophylaxis (hyperhydration, urate-lowering therapy) and bone marrow biopsy. Empirical steroids before diagnosis should be avoided.
key_points: Night bone pain + pallor + petechiae = exclude leukaemia.
Same-day paediatric oncology contact; tumour-lysis prophylaxis is the first inpatient task.
Avoid empirical steroids before tissue diagnosis.
explanation.pearls: Use the FBC, film and LDH to triage urgency: any child with night bone pain plus a circulating blast count needs same-day oncology contact, not a 'review in a week' plan.

stem: You are the neonatal registrar called to see a 24-hour-old term boy whose mother had an antenatal anomaly scan showing bilateral hydronephrosis and oligohydramnios in the third trimester. He has so far passed only a few drops of urine; the midwife reports a palpable distended bladder. He has mild respiratory distress on room air. There is no antenatal MRI; he was delivered by spontaneous vaginal delivery in a regional centre with a paediatric service and access to telehealth subspecialty advice.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8°C, HR 160, RR 64, SpO2 95% on room air
data_table.Examination: Distended bladder palpable to umbilicus, weak urinary stream when bladder pressed, no abdominal masses, no respiratory crackles
data_table.Investigations: Bedside bladder US confirms gross bladder distension and bilateral hydroureteronephrosis; Cr 110 micromol/L, K 5.4 mmol/L, urine output last 12 h 0.5 mL/kg/h

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Pass a 6 Fr feeding tube to drain the bladder and arrange urgent paediatric urology transfer
options.rationale: Initial management of suspected posterior urethral valves is bladder drainage with a small feeding-tube catheter (Foleys may traumatise the prostatic urethra) followed by transfer for micturating cystourethrogram and cystoscopic valve ablation. (Source: APSA and ANZUNS paediatric urology guidance)

options.text: Defer any intervention and recheck serum creatinine and bladder volume in 24 hours time
options.rationale: Persistent obstruction worsens AKI, electrolyte derangement and bladder damage; drainage is required within hours. (Source: ANZUNS)

options.text: Commence IV furosemide 1 mg/kg now to maximise urine output before any catheter placement
options.rationale: Furosemide will not bypass a mechanical obstruction and can precipitate electrolyte derangement; the lesion is anatomical. (Source: ANZUNS)

options.text: Insert a 10 Fr Foley balloon catheter to maintain continuous drainage during transport in retrieval
options.rationale: A 10 Fr Foley balloon catheter can sit in the prostatic urethra and traumatise the valves; a fine feeding tube is preferred until cystoscopic assessment. (Source: ANZUNS)

options.text: Perform a single suprapubic aspiration only and discharge to outpatient urology follow up
options.rationale: A single aspiration does not provide ongoing drainage; an infant with PUV needs continuous bladder decompression and definitive surgical valve ablation. (Source: ANZUNS)

explanation.summary: Posterior urethral valves are the commonest cause of severe obstructive uropathy in newborn boys and typically present with antenatal hydronephrosis, oligohydramnios and a distended bladder with weak stream postnatally. Initial management is bladder drainage with a small feeding-tube catheter (Foley balloons can traumatise the prostatic urethra), followed by paediatric urology transfer for MCUG and cystoscopic ablation. Renal function should be monitored as nephrogenic damage may already be established.
key_points: Fine feeding-tube catheter for initial drainage, not a Foley.
Definitive treatment is cystoscopic valve ablation.
Renal failure, pulmonary hypoplasia and bladder dysfunction may persist long-term.
explanation.pearls: Antenatal bilateral hydronephrosis + oligohydramnios + male fetus = plan for postnatal PUV; have a 6 Fr feeding tube ready at birth and paediatric urology on standby.

stem: You are the GP responding to a code-blue call at a community health centre. A 9-month-old girl was being fed grated apple and is now in obvious respiratory distress with an ineffective weak cough, marked stridor and central cyanosis. She has been unable to vocalise for 20 seconds. A nurse is supporting her in a head-down prone position on the resuscitator's forearm. She has no known allergies and is fully immunised. There are no signs of trauma.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 70 bpm, falling, SpO2 70%, RR ineffective, central cyanosis
data_table.Examination: Unable to cry or cough, marked indrawing, decreased air entry bilaterally, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Not applicable in the acute setting

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate manoeuvre?

options.text: Perform a finger sweep across the oropharynx to remove any directly visualised foreign body
options.rationale: Blind finger sweeps may push the foreign body further into the airway; only remove a foreign body that you can directly visualise. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Commence chest compressions at the 30:2 ratio immediately because the heart rate is below 80
options.rationale: While bradycardia in choking is ominous, the immediate priority remains relief of the airway obstruction with back blows then chest thrusts; CPR commences only if the infant becomes unresponsive. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Perform five abdominal thrusts (Heimlich manoeuvre) just above the umbilicus in seated infant
options.rationale: Abdominal thrusts are contraindicated under 1 year because they risk visceral and hepatic injury; chest thrusts are used instead after back blows. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Deliver up to five firm back blows between the scapulae in a head-down prone position
options.rationale: In an infant under 1 year with ineffective cough and obstruction, ANZCOR guidance is to deliver up to five back blows in a head-down prone position followed by up to five chest thrusts; abdominal thrusts are contraindicated in infants. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Administer 100% oxygen via tight-fitting non-rebreather mask and observe for spontaneous relief
options.rationale: Oxygen cannot reach the alveoli through a fully obstructed airway; mechanical relief of the obstruction is the priority. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

explanation.summary: ANZCOR Guideline 4 directs that an infant under 1 year with ineffective cough and severe airway obstruction receives up to five back blows in a head-down prone position, followed by up to five chest thrusts if obstruction persists. Abdominal thrusts are contraindicated under 1 year. If the infant becomes unresponsive, commence infant CPR. Direct laryngoscopy with Magill forceps is the next step if available.
key_points: Back blows then chest thrusts in infants under 1 year.
Abdominal thrusts are contraindicated under 1 year.
Avoid blind finger sweeps.
explanation.pearls: Bradycardia precedes arrest in airway obstruction; treat the obstruction first - oxygen will not bypass a complete mechanical block.

stem: You are the neonatology fellow planning autumn discharge for an ex-29-week preterm girl, now 6 months corrected age and 4.8 kg, with established bronchopulmonary dysplasia requiring 0.25 L/min home oxygen at discharge. Her family lives in regional Victoria. RSV season is about to start in 4 weeks. Her older brother attends day care and the family includes a 2-year-old cousin sharing the household. Her mother asks what can be done to reduce her risk of severe bronchiolitis this season.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7°C, HR 130, RR 48 on 0.25 L/min via nasal prongs, SpO2 95%
data_table.Examination: Weight 4.8 kg (3rd centile), mild intercostal recession, scattered fine inspiratory crackles, no acute respiratory distress
data_table.Investigations: Maternal antenatal RSV vaccination was not given (vaccine eligibility had not been reached before delivery), most recent chest X-ray: chronic interstitial changes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate prevention strategy?

options.text: Commence oral montelukast 4 mg nightly to reduce post-bronchiolitis wheeze across the season
options.rationale: Montelukast is not an RSV prophylactic and does not prevent severe RSV bronchiolitis admission. (Source: GINA Australia and Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Administer monthly intramuscular palivizumab for the duration of the local RSV season
options.rationale: Palivizumab is being replaced by single-dose nirsevimab in Australian jurisdictions because nirsevimab provides season-long protection in one dose. (Source: AIH)

options.text: Single weight-based intramuscular nirsevimab dose before the start of the RSV season
options.rationale: AU NIRP (immunisation handbook) recommends single-dose nirsevimab for high-risk infants under 2 years with chronic lung disease of prematurity or significant CHD entering their first or second RSV season, replacing monthly palivizumab in most jurisdictions. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook NIRP)

options.text: Standard hygiene and exposure-avoidance advice without any specific RSV pharmacoprophylaxis
options.rationale: Behavioural measures alone are insufficient in an infant with BPD on home oxygen; targeted monoclonal-antibody prophylaxis substantially reduces hospital admission. (Source: AIH)

options.text: Vaccinate the infant with the maternal recombinant RSV vaccine RSVpreF intramuscular dose
options.rationale: RSVpreF is a maternal vaccine given antenatally; it is not licensed for direct infant vaccination. (Source: AIH)

explanation.summary: Nirsevimab is a long-acting monoclonal antibody recommended in Australia for infants entering their first RSV season and for high-risk infants (chronic lung disease of prematurity, haemodynamically significant congenital heart disease) entering their second RSV season. A single weight-based IM dose covers the entire season, replacing monthly palivizumab where funded. Maternal vaccination with RSVpreF is the alternative pathway for healthy term babies whose mothers were eligible antenatally.
key_points: Single-dose nirsevimab covers the whole RSV season.
Eligible: first season for all infants, second season for high-risk infants.
Maternal RSVpreF in pregnancy is an alternative for healthy term infants.
explanation.pearls: If maternal RSVpreF was given in pregnancy at 28-36 weeks, the term infant does not also need nirsevimab; if not, a single nirsevimab dose is the AU default.

subtopic_detail: Anti-D in threatened miscarriage before 12 weeks
stem: You are the GP seeing a 27-year-old Rh-negative G2P1 woman at 9+3 weeks of pregnancy with one day of light vaginal spotting. She had a normal vaginal delivery 18 months ago with anti-D given postpartum. Today she is haemodynamically stable, the cervix is closed on speculum, and ultrasound shows a single intrauterine pregnancy with fetal heart pulsation and a small subchorionic haematoma. Her partner is also Rh-negative according to historical records.

data_table.Examination: Closed cervix, small amount of dark old blood in vault, no cervical excitation, uterus appropriate size
data_table.Investigations: Bedside US: viable intrauterine pregnancy 9+3 weeks, small subchorionic haematoma, no products in cervical canal; blood group O Rh-negative confirmed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management regarding anti-D?

options.text: Do not administer anti-D because the gestation is under 12 weeks without instrumentation
options.rationale: RANZCOG guidance is that anti-D is not required for spontaneous threatened miscarriage before 12 weeks; it is required if there is bleeding plus uterine instrumentation, ectopic pregnancy, molar pregnancy or any event at or after 12 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin)

options.text: Administer 250 IU anti-D immunoglobulin intramuscularly within 72 hours of the bleeding
options.rationale: 250 IU is the standard dose used for events under 12 weeks where anti-D is indicated (eg surgical management of miscarriage), but no anti-D is needed for spontaneous threatened miscarriage without instrumentation. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Confirm partner Rh-negative status on group and screen before giving any anti-D dose
options.rationale: Paternal Rh status is unreliable in clinical decision-making and does not negate the need for prophylaxis when otherwise indicated; the decision rests on maternal Rh-D status and clinical event. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Administer 625 IU anti-D immunoglobulin intramuscularly within 72 hours of the bleeding
options.rationale: 625 IU is the standard dose used from 12 weeks onward; it is not indicated at 9+3 weeks for a threatened miscarriage. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Send a Kleihauer-Betke test now to quantify any feto-maternal haemorrhage before anti-D
options.rationale: Kleihauer is used to quantify large feto-maternal haemorrhage in later pregnancy to guide additional anti-D dosing; it does not change first-trimester management. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Anti-D immunoglobulin is given to non-sensitised Rh-D-negative pregnant women after any sensitising event from 12 weeks gestation, and before 12 weeks only if there is instrumentation, ectopic pregnancy, molar pregnancy, or significant bleeding. Spontaneous threatened miscarriage before 12 weeks does not require anti-D. Doses are 250 IU before 12 weeks and 625 IU from 12 weeks; Kleihauer is added after 20 weeks for quantification.
key_points: Anti-D not required for spontaneous threatened miscarriage under 12 weeks.
Anti-D required under 12 weeks if instrumentation, ectopic or molar pregnancy.
Standard antenatal anti-D prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks remains separately scheduled.
explanation.pearls: Do not give anti-D for first-trimester spontaneous threatened miscarriage; the indication starts with instrumentation, ectopic, molar pregnancy, or any event after 12 weeks.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 32-year-old G1 woman at 34 weeks of pregnancy whose routine antenatal bloods show a platelet count of 112 x10^9/L. She is asymptomatic, blood pressure is normal and there is no rash, mucosal bleeding or splenomegaly. Her previous platelet counts during this pregnancy were 168 (booking) and 145 (28 weeks). She has no history of bleeding diathesis, no easy bruising, and is not on any medications.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height 34 cm, no bruising, no petechiae, no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 112 x10^9/L, otherwise FBC normal, LFTs normal, urate normal, urine PCR negative, blood film no schistocytes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure as gestational thrombocytopenia, continue routine antenatal care, recheck at 36 weeks
options.rationale: Gestational thrombocytopenia (5 to 10% of pregnancies) is the commonest cause of a mild platelet drop in the third trimester; it is benign, requires no treatment, and platelets above 100 are compatible with vaginal birth and neuraxial anaesthesia. (Source: HSANZ/THANZ Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy)

options.text: Administer IV immunoglobulin 1 g/kg now to raise the platelet count before delivery onset
options.rationale: IVIG is used for severe ITP with platelets below 50 or active bleeding; it is not indicated for mild gestational thrombocytopenia. (Source: HSANZ/THANZ)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily empirically for presumed immune thrombocytopenia
options.rationale: ITP usually presents with platelets below 100 earlier in pregnancy with no antenatal trend pointing to gestational thrombocytopenia; empirical steroids are not warranted without a clear ITP diagnosis. (Source: HSANZ/THANZ)

options.text: Arrange same-day delivery on the basis of the maternal platelet count and fetal safety risk
options.rationale: Delivery is not indicated for gestational thrombocytopenia and would expose mother and fetus to iatrogenic prematurity. (Source: HSANZ/THANZ)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section at 39 weeks because vaginal delivery is unsafe at these levels
options.rationale: Mode of delivery is determined by obstetric indications, not by gestational thrombocytopenia; spontaneous vaginal birth is safe with platelets above 100. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Gestational thrombocytopenia is the most common cause of mild thrombocytopenia in pregnancy, typically appearing in the third trimester with platelets above 70 and resolving postpartum. It does not affect neonatal platelet count and does not change mode of delivery. Distinguishing features from ITP are timing (later, milder) and absence of pre-pregnancy thrombocytopenia. Pre-eclampsia, HELLP, TTP, lupus and viral infection are alternative causes to exclude with BP, LFTs, urate, urine PCR and a blood film.
key_points: Platelets typically 70-150, third-trimester onset, resolves postpartum.
No treatment, no change to mode of delivery if platelets above 70.
Differential includes ITP, pre-eclampsia/HELLP, TTP.
explanation.pearls: Look at the antenatal platelet trend: gradual mild drop late in pregnancy is gestational; sudden drop or platelets under 70 needs ITP/HELLP work-up.

subtopic_detail: PCOS metformin in woman seeking pregnancy
stem: You are the GP seeing a 29-year-old woman with PCOS by Rotterdam criteria who has been trying to conceive for 12 months. She has BMI 32, oligomenorrhoea (cycles every 60 to 90 days), normal day-2 FSH and TSH, and a normal hysterosalpingogram. Her partner has a normal semen analysis. She has already lost 4 kg with exercise and dietary changes over 4 months and her glucose tolerance is currently normal but with an HbA1c of 38 mmol/mol. She wants to know what to do next.

data_table.Examination: Mild acne, mild hirsutism on chin and upper lip, no acanthosis nigricans, normal pelvic examination
data_table.Investigations: Normal FSH, LH, prolactin, TSH, normal pelvic US showing polycystic ovarian morphology, HbA1c 38 mmol/mol, OGTT normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological strategy to support conception?

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg from day 2 to day 6 of the cycle and monitor follicular response on US
options.rationale: Letrozole is now first-line for ovulation induction in PCOS in Australia, with higher live-birth rates than clomiphene and a lower multiple pregnancy rate. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS)
source_refs: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS (AU lead)

options.text: Refer directly for IVF treatment cycle planning before any oral ovulation-induction trial
options.rationale: IVF is reserved for women who do not conceive after a reasonable trial of letrozole-based ovulation induction or those with co-existing tubal or sperm factors. (Source: International PCOS guideline)
source_refs: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS (AU lead)

options.text: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg from day 2 to day 6 of the cycle and monitor follicular response
options.rationale: Clomiphene was historically first-line but the AU-led international guideline now places letrozole first-line because of superior live-birth rates and a lower multiple pregnancy rate. (Source: International PCOS guideline)
source_refs: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS (AU lead)

options.text: Refer for laparoscopic ovarian drilling as second-line surgical before any oral therapy
options.rationale: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling is a second-line option after failure of ovulation induction; it is not first-line because of anaesthetic risk and possible reduction in ovarian reserve. (Source: International PCOS guideline)
source_refs: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS (AU lead)

options.text: Metformin 500 mg twice daily as monotherapy and reassess after six months of treatment
options.rationale: Metformin is useful as an adjunct when there is insulin resistance or for cycle regulation but it is not first-line for ovulation induction in non-diabetic PCOS in women trying to conceive. (Source: International PCOS guideline)
source_refs: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS (AU lead)

explanation.summary: Letrozole is the first-line ovulation-induction agent for women with PCOS who want to conceive, based on better live-birth rates and a lower multiple pregnancy rate than clomiphene. Lifestyle change remains the foundation. Metformin may be considered as an adjunct in obesity or insulin resistance or in combination with letrozole in resistant cases. Laparoscopic ovarian drilling and IVF are second-line.
key_points: Letrozole 2.5 mg day 2-6 is first-line.
Metformin is adjunctive, not first-line.
Lifestyle weight loss remains foundational.
explanation.pearls: Start letrozole at 2.5 mg and titrate up to 7.5 mg over three cycles if anovulatory; track with ultrasound and mid-luteal progesterone.

sources.label: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS (AU lead)
